title: 帷幕
summary:
Barbara Franklin tells Hercule Poirot that she feels like a burden to her husband, Dr. John Franklin, suggesting she wishes to "end it all" to free him. Hastings relays this comment to Nurse Crewe, who dismisses it as non-literal. Later, while sitting by a beech tree near the laboratory, Barbara adopts a different persona, presenting herself as an adoring wife. She describes Dr. Franklin as a "saint" who is dangerously dedicated to scientific advancement, noting that he once performed a risky gas experiment on himself. She expresses fear that he might attempt to perform his current "bean experiment" on himself to obtain human data.

After Barbara leaves to accompany Boyd Carleton to view a house, Hastings observes that she seems to enjoy playing various dramatic roles. When Nurse Clyde arrives to return Barbara's forgotten handbag and gloves, Hastings remarks on Barbara's perceived irresponsibility and lack of consideration for the staff. Poirot responds cryptically to this remark by noting Nurse Clyde's red-brown hair.

The following day during lunch, Hastings, Judith, Boyd Cartwright, and Norton engage in a discussion regarding the ethics of euthanasia. Hastings expresses concern that the practice could grant excessive power to a patient's relatives. Norton argues that euthanasia should only be used for incurable illnesses at the willing request of the patient. Boyd Cartwright challenges the concept of a patient's true willingness to die, recounting an anecdote about a cancer patient who was given morphine by a doctor but ultimately chose not to take a lethal dose, arguing that people generally prefer to stay alive.
